metadata.title: Guideline on quality, non-clinical and clinical requirements for investigational advanced therapy medicinal products in clinical trials
metadata.notes: Major 60-page CAT guideline adopted December 2024, effective 1 July 2025. Covers quality, non-clinical, and clinical requirements for investigational ATMPs. Supersedes earlier guidance. Cross-references EMA/CAT/80183/2014 for gene therapy MAA requirements.

rules.rule_text: Investigational ATMPs must be produced in accordance with the Guidelines on Good Manufacturing Practice specific to Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (EudraLex Volume 4, Part IV). GMP compliance is a prerequisite for conduct of clinical trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: Compliance with GMP requirements as laid down in the Guidelines on Good Manufacturing Practice specific to Advanced Therapy Medicinal Products (EudraLex Volume 4, Part IV) is a prerequisite for the conduct of clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Products containing or consisting of genetically modified organisms (GMOs) must comply with GMO legislation under Directives 2001/18/EC and/or 2009/41/EC.
rules.verbatim_quote: For those products consisting of, or containing, genetically modified organisms (GMOs) compliance with the GMO legislation is required (Directives 2001/18/EC and/or 2009/41/EC).

rules.rule_text: Donation, procurement, and testing of human cell-based products must comply with Directive 2004/23/EC or where applicable Regulation (EU) 2024/1938. Bidirectional traceability from donor to recipient must be ensured. Data must be kept for 30 years after the expiry date of the product unless a longer time period is required in the clinical trial authorisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Donation, procurement, and testing of human cell-based products need to comply with the requirements of Directive 2004/23/EC or where applicable Directive 2002/98/EC (repealed by Regulation (EC) 2024/1938 from 7 August 2027). The traceability from the recipient of the product to the donor of the cells or tissues should be ensured. The traceability system should be bidirectional (from donor to recipient and from recipient to donor). Data should be kept for 30 years after the expiry date of the product, unless a longer time period is required in the clinical trial authorisation.
rules.exceptions: Longer retention period may be required per clinical trial authorisation

rules.rule_text: A risk-based approach must be applied throughout the development of an investigational ATMP. An initial risk analysis must be performed at the beginning of product development based on existing knowledge on the type of investigational product and its intended use. The risk analysis must be updated throughout the product life cycle as new data become available. Key points relevant to the risk analysis must be summarised in the IMPD.
rules.verbatim_quote: Throughout the development of an ATMP, a risk-based approach can be applied. The extent of the quality, non-clinical and clinical data can be adapted having regard to the identified potential risks. In particular, the sponsor can perform at the beginning of product development an initial risk analysis based on existing knowledge on the type of investigational product and its intended use... The risk analysis should be updated by the applicant throughout the product life cycle as new data become available. Key points relevant to the understanding of the product development approach chosen, should be summarized in the IMPD.

rules.rule_text: Quality data for investigational ATMPs should be presented in the IMPD following the CTD structure (Module 3), divided into active substance (AS) and finished product (FP) sections. The active substance, any intermediate and the finished product should be identified, if possible.
rules.verbatim_quote: The data on quality aspects of investigational ATMPs should be presented in a logical structure, ideally according to the specified structure of a common technical document (CTD) such as that of Module 3. The data submitted in this module should be consistent with and complement other parts of the clinical trial submission package. The IMPD should be divided into active substance (AS) and finished product (FP) sections.
rules.exceptions: For continuous manufacturing processes, applicant may choose where to provide required information

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory clinical trials must be conducted with a product based on a manufacturing process that is as mature as feasible. Introduction of substantial manufacturing changes during pivotal clinical studies is not recommended due to comparability issues at MAA.
rules.verbatim_quote: Confirmatory clinical trials should be conducted with a product based on a manufacturing process that is as mature as feasible. The introduction of substantial changes during pivotal clinical studies is not recommended as this will give rise to comparability issues at MAA, a particular challenge for ATMPs.

rules.rule_text: For gene therapy investigational ATMP active substances, a description of the vector, its structural features and the genetic construct must be provided, including a diagrammatic representation. The therapeutic sequence(s), junction regions and regulatory elements must be provided. Any sequence added for targeting, regulation or expression must be described.
rules.verbatim_quote: For gene therapy investigational ATMP active substance, a description of the vector, its structural features and the genetic construct should be provided, including a diagrammatic representation. The therapeutic sequence(s), junction regions and regulatory elements should be provided. Any sequence which has been added for targeting, regulation or expression of the genetic construct should be described.

rules.rule_text: The manufacturing process and process controls for investigational ATMPs must be carefully designed, described concisely and step-by-step. A flow chart of all successive manufacturing steps following entry of the starting material must be provided, including relevant process parameters and in-process control testing. All relevant processing and hold times must be specified.
rules.source_section: S.2.2. Description of manufacturing process and process controls
rules.verbatim_quote: The manufacturing process and process controls should be carefully designed, described concisely and step-by-step. A flow chart of all successive manufacturing steps following the entry of the starting material in the manufacturing process including relevant process parameters and in-process control testing linked to these steps should be indicated. All relevant processing and hold times should be specified.

rules.rule_text: The manufacturing process must be set up to minimise the risk of microbiological contamination. Procedures to minimize microbial ingress must be described. Routine IPC testing for microbial contamination during manufacture must be performed.
rules.source_section: S.2.2. Description of manufacturing process and process controls
rules.verbatim_quote: The manufacturing process must be set up to minimise the risk of microbiological contamination. The procedures implemented to minimize microbial ingress should be described, such as incoming goods qualification and testing, 0.2 µm filtration of media and supplement into culture vessels, as well as routine IPC testing for microbial contamination during manufacture.

rules.rule_text: For replication-deficient viral vectors, a suitable in-process test is essential to show that replication-competent viruses (RCV) are below an acceptable level during production. The absence, or a justified and minimised upper limit, of RCV must be demonstrated using a suitably validated assay.
rules.source_section: S.2.2. Description of manufacturing process and process controls
rules.verbatim_quote: A suitable in process test is essential to show that replication-competent viruses (RCV) are below an acceptable level during production. For replication-deficient viral vectors, the absence or a justified and minimised upper limit of RCV should be demonstrated using a suitably validated assay (and information provided in S.4.2. and S.4.3.).

rules.rule_text: Any reprocessing during manufacture of the active substance must be described and justified. Reprocessing can only be considered in exceptional circumstances and is usually restricted to re-filtration and re-concentration steps upon technical failure of equipment or mechanical breakdown.
rules.source_section: S.2.2. Description of manufacturing process and process controls
rules.verbatim_quote: Any reprocessing during manufacture of the active substance (e.g. filter integrity test failure) should be described and justified. Reprocessing could only be considered in exceptional circumstances. These situations are usually restricted to re-filtration and re-concentration steps upon technical failure of equipment or mechanical breakdown of a chromatography column.

rules.rule_text: Where cell lines are used as starting materials, an appropriately characterised Master Cell Bank (MCB) and Working Cell Bank (WCB) must be established, whenever possible. Generation and characterisation of cell banks must follow ICH Q5D and relevant Ph. Eur. texts. An MCB must be established prior to initiation of exploratory trials.
rules.source_section: S.2.3. Controls of materials — C. Banking system for established cell lines
rules.verbatim_quote: Where cell lines are used as starting materials, an appropriately characterised Master Cell Bank (MCB) and Working Cell Bank (WCB) should be established, whenever possible. Information on the cell banking process and characterisation and testing of the established cell banks should be provided as well as available information on cell substrate stability. The generation and characterisation of the cell banks should be performed in accordance with principles of ICH Q5D and relevant Ph. Eur. texts. While a MCB should be established prior to the initiation of exploratory trials, the WCB may not always be established early on.
rules.exceptions: WCB may not always be established early on

rules.rule_text: The presence of antibiotic resistance genes in an investigational GTMP must be avoided given the burden of bacterial multi-resistance to antibiotics. If unavoidable, a risk analysis must be provided. The presence of antibiotic resistance genes in plasmids used in production of starting materials is generally considered justifiable only if adequate clearance can be demonstrated.
rules.source_section: S.2.3. Controls of materials — Starting materials for investigational GTMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: The presence of antibiotic resistance genes in an investigational GTMP should be avoided given the burden of bacterial multi-resistance to antibiotics and the existence of alternative methods for selection. If unavoidable, a risk analysis should be provided. The presence of antibiotic resistance genes in plasmids used in the production of starting materials (i.e., mRNA, viral vectors, recombinant proteins) is generally considered justifiable, provided the manufacturer can demonstrate adequate clearance of antibiotic resistance gene-containing DNA from the manufacturing process.
rules.exceptions: Plasmid-based antibiotic resistance genes acceptable if adequate clearance demonstrated

rules.rule_text: Control of virus seed banks must include identity (genetic and immunological), virus concentration and infectious titre, genome integrity, phenotypic characteristics, transcription/expression/biological activity of the therapeutic sequence, sterility (bacterial and fungal), absence of mycoplasma, absence of adventitious/contaminating virus and replication-competent virus, and absence of bacteriophages where vectors are produced on bacterial substrates.
rules.source_section: S.2.3. Controls of materials — A. Virus seed banks
rules.verbatim_quote: Control of virus seed banks (including genetically modified phages or phage-like particles designed to transduce therapeutic sequence in bacteria) should include identity (genetic and immunological), virus concentration and infectious titre, genome integrity, phenotypic characteristics, transcription/expression/biological activity of the therapeutic sequence as applicable, sterility (bacterial, and fungal), absence of mycoplasma, absence of adventitious/contaminating virus and replication-competent virus (where the product is replication-deficient or replication-conditional), absence of bacteriophages (where vectors are produced on bacterial substrates). The sequence of key elements such as the therapeutic and the regulatory elements should be confirmed.

rules.rule_text: For cell-based investigational ATMPs, the cellular component must be characterised at minimum in terms of identity, purity, impurities (see S.3.2.), viability, quantity (cell number), and potency.
rules.source_section: S.3.1. Elucidation of structure and other characteristics — Characterisation studies of cell-based investigational ATMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: At minimum characterisation of the cellular component should be established in terms of identity, purity, impurities (see also S.3.2.), viability, quantity (cell number) and potency.

rules.rule_text: A suitable potency assay must be in place when material for the first-in-human (FIH) clinical trial is produced. In vivo potency tests should generally be avoided and, where unavoidable, must be justified according to 3R principles. In vivo potency tests should be replaced by in vitro tests whenever possible prior to confirmatory clinical trials.
rules.source_section: S.3.1. Elucidation of structure and other characteristics
rules.verbatim_quote: It is strongly recommended that suitable methods to quantitatively measure the biological activity are developed as soon as possible. A suitable potency assay should be in place when material for the FIH clinical trial is produced. In vivo potency tests should generally be avoided. Where in vivo tests are unavoidable, they must be justified according to 3R principles. In vivo potency tests should be replaced by in vitro tests whenever possible prior to confirmatory clinical trials.
rules.exceptions: In vivo tests acceptable if justified per 3R principles

rules.rule_text: For investigational GTMPs, characterisation tests performed on the vector must, as a minimum, include identity (desired transgene and vector) and purity. For viral vectors, titre and infectivity potential should normally be determined. The presence/absence of other genetic features with potential safety impact such as immunomodulatory CpG sequences must be determined unless otherwise justified.
rules.source_section: S.3.1 — 2. Characterisation studies of investigational GTMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: Tests performed on vector should as a minimum include identity (desired transgene and vector) and purity. For viral vectors, titre and infectivity potential should normally be determined. The presence/absence of other genetic features with potential impact on safety such as immunomodulatory CpG sequences should be determined, unless otherwise justified.
rules.exceptions: CpG sequences may be justified to omit

rules.rule_text: For genetically modified cells, in vitro assays for transduction efficiency and vector copy number per transduced cell must be conducted. For genome editing tools, in vitro assays for editing efficiency and off-target editing must be evaluated by in vitro and in silico methods. Homogeneity and genetic stability of genetically modified cells must be thoroughly characterised.
rules.source_section: S.3.1 — 2. Characterisation studies of investigational GTMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: For genetically modified cells, in vitro assays for transduction efficiency and vector copy number per transduced cell should be conducted. A risk-based approach should be followed to determine the need for integration site characterization. For genetically modified cells derived using genome editing tools, in vitro assays for editing efficiency and off-target editing should be evaluated by in vitro and in silico methods. In addition, the cells should be analysed for large DNA-fragment inversions, deletions, duplications or chromosomal rearrangements. Homogeneity and genetic stability of genetically modified cells should be thoroughly characterised.
rules.exceptions: Integration site characterization by risk-based approach

rules.rule_text: In the case of replication-competent or conditionally replicating viral vectors, it must be demonstrated that there is no inclusion of known oncogenic/tumorigenic sequences, and that the infectivity, virulence and pathogenicity of the RCV are characterized after the desired genetic manipulations.
rules.source_section: S.3.1 — 2. Characterisation studies of investigational GTMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: It should be demonstrated that there is no inclusion of known oncogenic/tumorigenic sequences, and that if the parental viral strain is a known pathogen, the infectivity, virulence and pathogenicity of the RCV should be characterized after the desired genetic manipulations.

rules.rule_text: For vectors designed to be replication-deficient or conditionally replicating, the overall absence of replication-competent virus or a justified and minimised upper limit must be demonstrated and/or conditional replication demonstrated. Helper or hybrid viruses generated during manufacture must be eliminated or minimized.
rules.verbatim_quote: In the case of vectors designed to be replication-deficient or conditionally replicating, the overall absence of replication-competent virus or a justified and minimised upper limit should be demonstrated and/or conditional replication demonstrated. Helper or hybrid viruses generated or used during manufacture or components of the production system should be eliminated or minimized.
rules.exceptions: Justified minimised upper limit acceptable as alternative to absence

rules.rule_text: Cell viability specifications must be set and justified for content of non-viable cells, such as the ratio of non-viable to viable cells, % viability, or a limit of the total number of non-viable cells per dose, because cell viability is a critical parameter directly correlated to biologic activity in most cases.
rules.verbatim_quote: Irrespective of the cell type, the cell population can contain non-viable cells. Since cell viability is an important parameter for product integrity and is directly correlated to the biologic activity in most cases, a specification should be set, and justified, for the content of non-viable cells, such as the ratio of non-viable to viable cells, % viability (of total cells/intended cell population, if applicable) or a limit of the total number of non-viable cells per dose.

rules.rule_text: Specifications for active substance must include tests with defined acceptance criteria for quantity, identity, purity, microbiological assays, and biological activity. The use of 'record' or 'report results' without defined limits is not acceptable for test parameters relevant to safety.
rules.verbatim_quote: Specifications should be meaningful and quantitative and the use of 'record' or 'report results' should be avoided whenever possible. For test parameters relevant to safety, the absence of defined limits is not acceptable. Tests and defined acceptance criteria are expected for quantity, identity, purity, microbiological assays and biological activity.
rules.exceptions: Absence of quantitative limits for potency/biological activity at FIH trial could exceptionally be accepted if sufficient control justified

rules.rule_text: All safety-relevant analytical methods such as those used for microbiological and viral testing must be fully validated prior to the start of the clinical trial, irrespective of clinical trial phase. Validations of sterility and microbial assays, as well as RCV testing, are required for all clinical trial phases.
rules.verbatim_quote: Irrespective of the clinical trial phase, all safety relevant methods such as those used for microbiological and viral testing have to be fully validated prior to the start of the clinical trial. The suitability of the analytical methods used for viral testing, either as a qualitative or a quantitative method, should be substantiated. ICH Q5A (R2) should be considered. Chapter 3.2 'Recommended Viral Detection and Identification Assays' is applicable. Validations of sterility and microbial assays, as well as RCV testing are required for all clinical trial phases.

rules.rule_text: For investigational GTMPs, the genetic identity and integrity of the active substance must be assured. Tests must identify both the therapeutic sequence and the vector. An assessment of the ratio of infectious to physical particles in the case of viral vectors is expected.
rules.source_section: S.4.1. Specification — Additional information for confirmatory clinical trials
rules.verbatim_quote: In case of investigational GTMPs, where applicable, the genetic identity and integrity of the active substance should be assured. Tests should identify both the therapeutic sequence, the vector and, if applicable and possible, the complexed nucleic acid sequences. In addition to sequencing data, the identity of the active substance may also be confirmed through infection/transduction assays and detection of expression/activity of the therapeutic sequence(s). An assessment of the ratio of infectious to physical particles in the case of viral vectors is expected.

rules.rule_text: A stability protocol covering the proposed storage period and storage conditions of the active substance must be provided, including specifications with suitable limits, analytical methods and test intervals. A potency assay must be included in the stability protocol unless otherwise justified. The re-test period (as defined in ICH Q1A) is not applicable to ATMPs.
rules.verbatim_quote: A stability protocol covering the proposed storage period and storage conditions of the active substance should be provided, including specifications with suitable limits, analytical methods and test intervals. Unless justified, the testing interval should follow ICH Q5C. The re-test period (as defined in ICH Q1A guideline) is not applicable to ATMPs.
rules.exceptions: Re-test period concept not applicable to ATMPs

rules.rule_text: Shelf-life extension of the active substance beyond the period covered by real-time stability data is subject to limits: the maximum extension must not be more than double, or more than twelve months longer than the period covered by real-time stability data. Extension beyond the intended duration of long-term stability studies is not acceptable.
rules.verbatim_quote: The maximum shelf-life after the extension should not be more than double, or more than twelve months longer than the period covered by real time stability data obtained with representative batch(es). However, extension of the shelf life beyond the intended duration of the long-term stability studies is not acceptable
rules.exceptions: Not applicable to cell-based investigational ATMPs for accelerated studies

rules.rule_text: For finished product (investigational medicinal product) control, tests for content, identity and purity are mandatory. Tests for sterility and endotoxin are mandatory for sterile products. Mycoplasma testing is required for investigational cell-based ATMPs. A potency test must be included unless otherwise justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Tests for content, identity and purity are mandatory. Tests for sterility and endotoxin are mandatory for sterile products. Mycoplasma testing is required for investigational cell-based ATMPs. A potency test should be included unless otherwise justified (see S.4.1). For non-viral particles the specific transfection efficiency should be determined.
rules.exceptions: Potency test may be omitted if otherwise justified

rules.rule_text: All materials of human or animal origin including cell culture media and medium supplements used in the manufacturing process must be identified. A thorough testing for the absence of bacteria, fungi, mycoplasma and endotoxin shall be performed at the level of the finished product following the risk-based approach. Mycoplasma testing should ideally be performed on the harvest of the last cultivation stage prior to further processing.
rules.verbatim_quote: A thorough testing for the absence of bacteria, fungi, mycoplasma and endotoxin shall be performed at the level of finished product following the risk-based approach and considering administration characteristics. However, testing for mycoplasma should be ideally performed on the harvest of the last cultivation stage prior to further processing e.g. lysis, filtration, washing or purification as post-treatment testing significantly increases the risk to not detect potential contamination with mycoplasma.

rules.rule_text: Viral safety evaluation must be conducted for investigational ATMPs. For products produced using continuous cell lines, testing for presence of adventitious viruses must follow ICH Q5A principles. For genetically modified viral vectors, manufacturing steps should incorporate viral clearance steps for adventitious, endogenous, and helper viruses without impacting the product.
rules.source_section: A.2. Adventitious agents safety evaluation — Viral safety
rules.verbatim_quote: Where applicable, information assessing the risk with respect to potential viral contamination should be provided in this section. Risk assessment should be performed according to Ph. Eur. 5.1.7. General Text on Viral Safety... When a continuous cell line is used in production, testing for presence of adventitious viruses should be conducted according to the principles of ICH guideline Q5A and Ph. Eur. 5.2.3 should be followed... For genetically modified viral vectors in scope of Guideline ICH Q5A (R2), steps should be incorporated into the manufacturing process, whenever possible, that enable sufficient viral clearance (for adventitious viruses, endogenous viruses, helper viruses) without impacting the product.

rules.rule_text: TSE agents risk must be addressed for investigational ATMPs. The Note for Guidance on Minimising the Risk of Transmitting Animal Spongiform Encephalopathy Agents (EMEA/410/01) and the CHMP/CAT position statement on Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease and ATMPs (EMA/CHMP/BWP/353632/2010) apply.
rules.source_section: A.2. Adventitious agents safety evaluation — TSE agents
rules.verbatim_quote: Detailed information should be provided on the avoidance and control of transmissible spongiform encephalopathy agents. This information can include, for example, certification and control of the production process, as appropriate for the material, process and agent. The Note for Guidance on Minimising the Risk of Transmitting Animal Spongiform Encephalopathy Agents via Human and Veterinary Medicinal Products (EMEA/410/01) in its current version and the CHMP/CAT position statement on Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease and advanced therapy medicinal products (EMA/CHMP/BWP/353632/2010) are applicable.

rules.rule_text: A Product Specification File must be maintained for each IMP at the respective manufacturing site and must be continually updated as the development proceeds, ensuring appropriate traceability to previous versions.
rules.source_section: 4. Quality documentation — Changes to the IMP
rules.verbatim_quote: In accordance with Good Manufacturing Practice, a Product Specification File should be maintained for each IMP/auxiliary medicinal product at the respective site and be continually updated as the development of the product proceeds, ensuring appropriate traceability to the previous versions.

rules.rule_text: Non-clinical studies for investigational ATMPs must be conducted with the most appropriate pharmacologically relevant in vitro and/or in vivo and/or in silico models. In vivo animal studies must be planned considering the 3Rs (reduction, replacement, refinement) principles. Animal testing should be replaced by in vitro, ex vivo or in silico studies where appropriate.
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, non-clinical studies should be done with the most appropriate pharmacologically relevant in vitro and/or in vivo and/or in silico models available. In vivo animal studies should be carefully planned to ensure generation of robust and meaningful data while considering the 3Rs (reduction, replacement, refinement) principles. Where appropriate, animal testing should be replaced by in vitro, ex vivo or in silico studies or a combination thereof.

rules.rule_text: Data to demonstrate proof-of-concept must normally be available before human exposure. The minimum non-clinical data package before FIH must include: support for proof-of-concept in a relevant non-clinical model; support for the use of administration route, application procedure and devices; support for selection of safe and biologically effective starting dose; and appropriate safety data.
rules.source_section: 5.6. Minimum non-clinical data requirements before first-in-human studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to specific characteristics of ATMPs, most non-clinical data should usually be available before first administration to humans... At a minimum, the following information should be available before human exposure: support for the proof-of-concept in a relevant non-clinical model; support for the use of administration route, application procedure and application devices; support of the selection of safe and biologically effective starting dose; appropriate safety data.
rules.exceptions: In exceptional cases where in vitro/ex vivo/in vivo data cannot be generated, a comprehensive risk assessment with mitigation measures required

rules.rule_text: Pivotal non-clinical safety studies are generally expected to conform with GLP principles. However, it is recognised that full GLP compliance may not always be possible due to specific characteristics of ATMPs.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is generally expected that pivotal non-clinical safety studies are carried out in conformity with the principles of GLP. However, it is recognised that, due to the specific characteristics of ATMPs, it would not always be possible to conduct these studies in full conformity with GLP.
rules.exceptions: Full GLP compliance may not always be possible for ATMPs
cross_references: EMA GLP principles in relation to ATMPs, 26 January 2017

rules.rule_text: The risk of germline transmission and modification must be explored before use in humans for investigational GTMPs. The extent of germline studies depends on the type of GTMP and its distribution to the gonads.
rules.verbatim_quote: The risk of germline transmission and modification should also be explored before use in humans (according to the Guideline on non-clinical testing for inadvertent germline transmission of gene transfer vectors (EMEA/273974/2005) and the above-mentioned ICH S12 guideline).

rules.rule_text: The tumourigenic and oncogenic potential must be investigated in relevant in vitro/in vivo models for neoplasm signals, oncogene activation or cell proliferation index before exposing humans to investigational ATMPs. Standard lifetime rodent carcinogenicity studies are usually not required.
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, the risk of tumour formation needs to be addressed before exposing humans. Standard lifetime rodent carcinogenicity studies are usually not required. However, depending on the type of product, the tumourigenic and oncogenic potential shall be investigated in relevant in vitro/in vivo models for neoplasm signals, oncogene activation or cell proliferation index.
rules.exceptions: Standard lifetime rodent carcinogenicity studies usually not required

rules.rule_text: Clinical trials with investigational ATMPs must comply with Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 (Clinical Trials Regulation). A clinical trial may be conducted only if the rights, safety, dignity and well-being of subjects are protected and prevail over all other interests; and the trial is designed to generate reliable and robust data.
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, the development of an ATMP should follow the same general principles as other medicinal products. This includes the legal requirement of Regulation (EU) No 536/2014 that a clinical trial may be conducted only if the rights, safety, dignity and well-being of subjects are protected and prevail over all other interests; and the trial is designed to generate reliable and robust data.

rules.rule_text: ATMP clinical trials are usually conducted in patients and not in healthy volunteers. For exploratory trials, the population may be more restricted; for confirmatory trials, populations must be representative of patients intended to be treated after marketing authorisation, and exclusion criteria must be minimised and fully justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Clinical trials involving investigational ATMPs are usually conducted in patients and not in healthy volunteers... confirmatory trials should be designed to ensure that the trial populations overall are representative of patients intended to be treated after obtaining a marketing authorisation and therefore exclusion criteria should be minimised and fully justified.

rules.rule_text: The clinical trial protocol and Investigator's Brochure (IB) must include an evaluation of the reproductive risk, the period of potential risk, and a justification for the duration of contraceptive measures. Contraceptive measures must be adapted to the risks of specific ATMP products.
rules.verbatim_quote: The protocol and the investigators brochure (IB) should include an evaluation of the reproductive risk including the period of potential risk and a justification for the duration of contraceptive measures. Contraceptive measures should be adapted to the risks of specific products (e.g. taking into account the duration of shedding for AAV-based products).
rules.exceptions: No contraceptive measures needed for male subjects with ATMPs showing no teratogenicity risk and no shedding into semen

rules.rule_text: For male subjects treated with an in vivo viral gene therapy, male barrier protection must be used during the time the virus is shed into the semen and for a period of three months (90 days) after there is no virus shed.
rules.source_section: 6.1.3. Contraceptive measures — Recommendations for male subjects
rules.verbatim_quote: In the case of male subjects who are treated with an in vivo viral gene therapy, male barrier protection should be used during the time the virus is shed into the semen and for a period of three months or 90 days after there is no virus shed.

rules.rule_text: A rationale for the selected starting dose, dose escalation scheme and dosing schedule must be provided in the trial protocol for exploratory ATM clinical trials. In the absence of a dose-finding study, the absence and the dose selected must be thoroughly justified in the study protocol.
rules.verbatim_quote: A rationale for the selected starting dose, dose escalation scheme (when applicable) and dosing schedule is required in the trial protocol... In case where a dose-finding study is not feasible, the absence of such study and the dose selected should be thoroughly justified accordingly in the study protocol.
rules.exceptions: Dose-finding study absence must be thoroughly justified

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must outline in the benefit-risk assessment section of the study protocol how known and potential risks are addressed and minimised. Respective risk minimisation measures must be implemented during the conduct of the study. For clinical trials with anticipated risks of late onset events (e.g. tumourigenicity), measures to ensure adequate follow-up and timely detection must be implemented.
rules.source_section: 6.1.1. Anticipated benefits and risks for trial participants
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should outline in the benefit-risk assessment section of the study protocol how known and potential risks are addressed and minimized. Respective risk minimisation measures should be implemented during the conduct of the study.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory/pivotal clinical trials for ATMPs should preferably use randomized controlled designs. Where reference therapies are not available, comparison to best supportive care or placebo is expected. If single or double blinding is not possible, this must be appropriately justified and measures to reduce potential bias must be included.
rules.verbatim_quote: Randomized controlled, comparative trials are preferable over single arm trials, or trials with external, historical controls, as they minimise confounding baseline variables, reduce bias and are better suitable to obtain an unbiased estimate of the treatment effect and safety. Where reference therapies are not available, comparison to best supportive care treatment based on investigator's choice or placebo is expected to provide evidence of efficacy... If single or double blinding is not possible, this should be appropriately justified (e.g. when surgical procedures are involved) and the trial design should include measures to reduce potential bias by partial blinding.
rules.exceptions: Single arm trial acceptable for pivotal evidence if justified — reference to EMA/CHMP/430688/2024

rules.rule_text: Long-term efficacy and safety follow-up must be implemented for patients treated with investigational ATMPs, starting with FIH trials. The duration of follow-up must be determined according to a risk-based approach based on non-clinical study results, mechanism of action, persistence, product nature, and age-specific considerations.
rules.verbatim_quote: The ATMP developers should ensure that patients enrolled in clinical trials (starting with FIH trials) are appropriately followed-up in order to generate long-term efficacy and safety data sufficient to support the MAA. The details of the follow-up should be defined in the respective clinical trial protocol(s).

rules.rule_text: When a medical device is used in combination with an ATMP in clinical trials, its regulatory status must be explicitly stated (e.g. whether CE marked for its intended purpose). Where the ATMP has an integral medical device component, the product is governed by medicines legislation and a CE mark is not required, but scientific data supporting the device component must be proportionate to its role.
rules.source_section: 7. ATMP-device combinations / P.7. Container closure system
rules.verbatim_quote: If a medical device is used, its regulatory status should be explicitly stated (e.g. whether it is CE marked for its intended purpose or not). If it is certified for the intended purpose the CE certification should be provided... Where the ATMP has an integral medical device component the overall product is governed by the medicines legislation and a CE mark is not required.

rules.rule_text: For in vivo genome editing medicinal products, on-target effects and the risk of off-target edits must be adequately addressed. In silico and in vitro analyses must be performed to evaluate possible off-target edits and other potential genotoxic effects, especially those which could affect regulatory elements or gene sequences with a biological impact.
rules.source_section: S.3.1 — 2. Characterisation studies of investigational GTMPs
rules.verbatim_quote: For in vivo genome editing medicinal products, on-target effects and the risk of off-target edits should be adequately addressed. Among others, in silico and in vitro analyses should be performed to evaluate possible off-target edits and other potential genotoxic effects, especially those which could affect regulatory elements or gene sequences which could have a biological impact.

rules.rule_text: For investigational GTMPs biodistribution must be assessed before FIH. The dosing used for biodistribution studies should equate to or exceed the anticipated maximum clinical dose level. The route of administration and treatment regimen must be representative of the clinical use. For replication-competent virus GTMPs, biodistribution studies must be designed to cover a potential second viremia as a result of replication in vivo.
rules.verbatim_quote: The dosing used for biodistribution studies should equate to or exceed the anticipated maximum clinical dose level. The route of administration and the treatment regimen (frequency and duration) should be representative for the clinical use... If the administered GTMP is a replication-competent virus, biodistribution studies should be designed to cover a potential second viremia as a result of replication of the virus in vivo.
rules.exceptions: Risk-based approach can determine need for biodistribution studies for non-genetically modified cells
